summary:
Overall Risk Level: Critical

Key Issues to Fix First:
1. Multiple critical secrets (AWS Access Key ID and Secret Key, Anthropic API key, Stripe secret key, Database password, JWT secret, GitHub token) are hardcoded directly in the source code. This poses a severe risk of unauthorized access and data breaches.
2. JWT secret hardcoded in code undermines cryptographic security, increasing risk of token forgery and unauthorized access.
3. Sensitive secrets are stored even in comments as base64-encoded strings, which provide no security and risk secret leakage.
4. All hardcoded secrets fall under OWASP’s Sensitive Data Exposure category, indicating poor secret management practices.

Quick Wins and Hardening Recommendations:
- Immediately remove all hardcoded secrets from code and comments.
- Use environment variables or dedicated secret management solutions (e.g., HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager) to handle all credentials and keys securely.
- Implement secure access controls and audit logging on secret vaults.
- Adopt automated scanning tools to detect hardcoded secrets before code merges.
- Enforce strict code reviews focused on secret handling to prevent future exposure.

Addressing these issues promptly is critical to prevent unauthorized access, data leaks, and potential financial or reputational damage.

findings.title: Hardcoded AWS Access Key ID
findings.description: AWS Access Key ID is hardcoded in the source code. This can lead to unauthorized access if the key is exposed or the code is leaked.
findings.recommendation: Use environment variables or secure secrets management solutions to handle AWS credentials.

findings.title: Hardcoded AWS Secret Access Key
findings.description: AWS Secret Access Key is hardcoded in the source code. This is a serious security risk as it can be exploited for unauthorized access.
findings.recommendation: Store secrets securely using environment variables or dedicated secret management tools.

findings.title: Hardcoded Anthropic API Key
findings.description: Anthropic API key is hardcoded in the source code, which risks unauthorized usage and secret leakage.
findings.recommendation: Keep API keys out of source code and load them securely from environment variables or secret vaults.

findings.title: Hardcoded Stripe Secret Key
findings.description: Stripe secret key is hardcoded in the source code, risking exposure of payment credentials and fraudulent transactions.
findings.recommendation: Use secure environment variables or secret management services to handle payment-related secrets.

findings.title: Hardcoded Database Password
findings.description: Database password is hardcoded in the code, which risks unauthorized database access.
findings.recommendation: Use environment variables or secret managers to supply database credentials securely.

findings.title: Hardcoded JWT Secret
findings.description: JWT secret is hardcoded in the source code, which compromises token security and can lead to unauthorized token forging.
findings.recommendation: Manage JWT secrets securely using environment variables or secret vaults.

findings.title: Hardcoded GitHub Token
findings.description: GitHub token is hardcoded which can lead to unauthorized repository or API access if leaked.
findings.recommendation: Handle tokens via secure environment or secret management tools, avoid embedding them directly in the code.

findings.title: Base64 Encoded Secret in Comment
findings.description: A base64-encoded Stripe secret key is present in a comment. Encoding secrets does not provide security and risks exposure.
findings.recommendation: Never store secrets in source code or comments, even if encoded. Use secure secret storage mechanisms.
